objects.name: CyberNetSec.io
objects.description: CyberNetSec.io is a cybersecurity intelligence platform providing timely, accurate, deduplicated, and actionable threat intelligence for security professionals. The platform aggregates, deduplicates, and enriches threat data from diverse public sources, mapping all threats to MITRE ATT&CK and D3FEND frameworks with operationally-focused analysis, detection guidance, and mitigation recommendations. Operated by security professionals, for the security community.

objects.name: Jason Gomes
objects.description: Cybersecurity practitioner and founder of CyberNetSec.io with over 10 years of specialized experience in security operations, threat intelligence, incident response, and security automation. Expertise spans SOAR/XSOAR orchestration, threat intelligence platforms, SIEM/UEBA analytics, and cyber fusion centers.

objects.name: Atomic Arch Actor
objects.description: The unnamed threat actor group behind the 'Atomic Arch' supply chain attack, responsible for hijacking orphaned Arch User Repository (AUR) packages to distribute infostealers and rootkits to developers.
goals: Harvest developer credentials, tokens, and SSH keys for further compromise of corporate networks and other supply chain attacks.

objects.name: Compromise Software Dependencies and Development Tools
objects.description: Attackers compromised the software supply chain by hijacking trusted but orphaned packages in the Arch User Repository (AUR) and modifying their build scripts to install malware.

objects.name: Unix Shell
objects.description: The compromised `PKGBUILD` file, a shell script, was used to execute malicious commands, specifically `npm install`, to download the next stage payload.

objects.name: Ingress Tool Transfer
objects.description: The malicious npm package `atomic-lockfile` was transferred from the public npm registry to the victim's machine using the `npm install` command executed by the compromised build script.

objects.name: Private Keys
objects.description: The Rust-based infostealer payload was designed to specifically search for and exfiltrate SSH private keys from developer machines.

objects.name: Rootkit
objects.description: The final payload included an eBPF rootkit to hide the malware's processes and network connections, providing stealth and persistence.

objects.name: atomic-lockfile
objects.description: A malicious npm package used as a dropper in the Atomic Arch campaign. It is installed via a compromised PKGBUILD file and deploys a Rust-based infostealer and an eBPF rootkit.

objects.name: js-digest
objects.description: A malicious npm package used as a dropper in the Atomic Arch campaign, similar to atomic-lockfile.

objects.name: lockfile-js
objects.description: A malicious npm package used as a dropper in the Atomic Arch campaign, similar to atomic-lockfile.

objects.name: Atomic Arch Infostealer
objects.description: A Rust-based infostealer deployed by the Atomic Arch campaign, designed to search for and exfiltrate sensitive developer-related information like SSH keys, AWS credentials, and kubeconfigs.

objects.name: Atomic Arch eBPF Rootkit
objects.description: An eBPF rootkit deployed by the Atomic Arch campaign to provide stealth and persistence by hiding its processes and network connections.

objects.name: npm
objects.description: The Node Package Manager (npm) was abused by the attackers to host malicious packages and used as the installation mechanism on the victim's machine via compromised build scripts.

objects.name: Bun
objects.description: The Bun runtime was used as an alternative to npm for installing malicious packages in a second wave of the Atomic Arch campaign.

objects.name: Atomic Arch
objects.description: A supply chain attack campaign targeting Arch Linux developers by hijacking orphaned Arch User Repository (AUR) packages to distribute malware via malicious npm packages.

objects.name: Malicious npm Registry Packages
objects.description: The public npm registry was used to host malicious packages like 'atomic-lockfile', which served as the delivery vehicle for the final infostealer and rootkit payloads.

objects.name: Atomic Arch Malicious npm Packages
objects.description: Detects the presence of malicious npm packages associated with the Atomic Arch campaign.
objects.x_netsecio_sigma_rule:
title: Atomic Arch Malicious npm Package File
id: 7c5d1e8a-9f0b-4c6d-8a3e-1b9c0f8d7e6a
status: experimental
description: Detects the presence of malicious npm package files associated with the Atomic Arch campaign.
references:
  - https://www.sonatype.com/blog/atomic-arch-npm-campaign-adds-malicious-dependency
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-06-13
tags:
  - attack.t1195.001
level: high
logsource:
  category: file_event
  product: linux
detection:
  selection:
    TargetFilename|contains:
      - '/atomic-lockfile/'
      - '/js-digest/'
      - '/lockfile-js/'
  condition: selection

objects.name: Suspicious npm or Bun Install from Build Script
objects.description: Detects a build script process (like makepkg) spawning a package manager (like npm or bun) to install packages, which is anomalous and indicative of the Atomic Arch TTP.
objects.pattern:
title: Suspicious npm or Bun Install from makepkg
id: f8a1b2c3-d4e5-4f6a-b7c8-d9e0f1a2b3c4
status: experimental
description: Detects 'makepkg' or a shell process spawning 'npm' or 'bun' to install packages, a TTP used in the Atomic Arch campaign to hijack AUR packages.
references:
  - https://www.sonatype.com/blog/atomic-arch-npm-campaign-adds-malicious-dependency
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-06-13
tags:
  - attack.t1195.001
  - attack.t1059.004
level: medium
logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: linux
detection:
  selection:
    ParentImage|endswith:
      - '/makepkg'
      - '/sh'
      - '/bash'
    Image|endswith:
      - '/npm'
      - '/bun'
    CommandLine|contains: ' install '
  condition: selection

objects.description: The unnamed threat actor group behind the 'Atomic Arch' supply chain attack, responsible for hijacking orphaned Arch User Repository (AUR) packages to distribute infostealers and rootkits to developers.
Developers using the Arch Linux operating system and its Arch User Repository (AUR) were the primary target of the Atomic Arch supply chain attack.
Attackers compromised the software supply chain by hijacking trusted but orphaned packages in the Arch User Repository (AUR) and modifying their build scripts to install malware.
The compromised `PKGBUILD` file, a shell script, was used to execute malicious commands, specifically `npm install`, to download the next stage payload.
The malicious npm package `atomic-lockfile` was transferred from the public npm registry to the victim's machine using the `npm install` command executed by the compromised build script.
A malicious npm package used as a dropper in the Atomic Arch campaign. It is installed via a compromised PKGBUILD file and deploys a Rust-based infostealer and an eBPF rootkit.
The Node Package Manager (npm) was abused by the attackers to host malicious packages and used as the installation mechanism on the victim's machine via compromised build scripts.
The Bun runtime was used as an alternative to npm for installing malicious packages in a second wave of the Atomic Arch campaign.
A Rust-based infostealer deployed by the Atomic Arch campaign, designed to search for and exfiltrate sensitive developer-related information like SSH keys, AWS credentials, and kubeconfigs.
An eBPF rootkit deployed by the Atomic Arch campaign to provide stealth and persistence by hiding its processes and network connections.
A malicious npm package used as a dropper in the Atomic Arch campaign. It is installed via a compromised PKGBUILD file and deploys a Rust-based infostealer and an eBPF rootkit.
A malicious npm package used as a dropper in the Atomic Arch campaign, similar to atomic-lockfile.
A malicious npm package used as a dropper in the Atomic Arch campaign, similar to atomic-lockfile.
The Rust-based infostealer payload was designed to specifically search for and exfiltrate SSH private keys from developer machines.
The final payload included an eBPF rootkit to hide the malware's processes and network connections, providing stealth and persistence.
A malicious npm package used as a dropper in the Atomic Arch campaign. It is installed via a compromised PKGBUILD file and deploys a Rust-based infostealer and an eBPF rootkit.
A supply chain attack campaign targeting Arch Linux developers by hijacking orphaned Arch User Repository (AUR) packages to distribute malware via malicious npm packages.

objects.name: 'Atomic Arch' Supply Chain Attack Hijacks Orphaned Linux Packages to Target Developers
objects.description:
## Executive Summary
A new and insidious supply chain attack, named 'Atomic Arch,' has been identified targeting developers using the Arch Linux ecosystem. First reported around June 11, 2026, the campaign involves threat actors taking control of legitimate, trusted software packages in the Arch User Repository (AUR) that have been abandoned by their original maintainers. The attackers then modify the package's build instructions (`PKGBUILD` file) to add a malicious post-install script. This script covertly downloads and executes a malicious package from the public **[npm](https://www.npmjs.com/)** registry, which in turn deploys credential-stealing malware. The attack is dangerous because it abuses the inherent trust users have in established AUR packages. Security firm **[Sonatype](https://www.sonatype.com)** has been tracking the campaign, which has already compromised dozens of packages and appears to be ongoing.

## Threat Overview
- **Campaign Name:** Atomic Arch
- **Target:** Developers using Arch Linux and the Arch User Repository (AUR).
- **Attack Vector:** Supply chain attack via hijacking of orphaned AUR packages.
- **Methodology:**
  1. Attacker identifies a trusted but orphaned AUR package.
  2. Attacker requests and is granted ownership of the package.
  3. Attacker modifies the `PKGBUILD` file, adding a malicious post-install command.
  4. A user installs or updates the trusted package, unknowingly executing the malicious command.
  5. The command fetches and installs a malicious npm package (e.g., `atomic-lockfile`).
  6. The npm package deploys the final payload: an infostealer and a rootkit.
- **Payload:** A Rust-based infostealer and an eBPF rootkit designed to harvest developer credentials (SSH keys, API tokens, etc.).

## Technical Analysis
This attack cleverly subverts the community-driven trust model of the AUR. Unlike typosquatting, where an attacker creates a new, similarly named package, this method inherits the reputation and userbase of a previously legitimate project.

The core of the attack lies in modifying the `PKGBUILD` file. A typical malicious addition might look like this in the `post_install()` function:

```bash
# Malicious command added by attacker
/usr/bin/npm install -g atomic-lockfile
```

When an unsuspecting user runs `makepkg -si` or uses an AUR helper to install/update the compromised package, this command is executed with their user privileges. The `atomic-lockfile` npm package, tracked by Sonatype as `Sonatype-2026-003775`, contains the final payload. Analysis shows it includes:
- **A Rust-based Infostealer:** This component is designed to search for and exfiltrate sensitive developer-related information, such as `.ssh` keys, `.aws` credentials, `.kube` configs, and browser data.
- **An eBPF Rootkit:** This provides stealth and persistence capabilities, allowing the malware to hide its processes and network connections from the system administrator.

The campaign has evolved, with a second wave on June 12 using the `Bun` runtime as an alternative to `npm` for installation, demonstrating the attacker's adaptability.

### MITRE ATT&CK TTPs
- **[`T1195.001 - Compromise Software Dependencies and Development Tools`](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1195/001/):** This is the core technique, compromising a trusted software repository.
- **[`T1059.004 - Unix Shell`](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1059/004/):** The `PKGBUILD` file uses shell commands to execute the malicious installation.
- **[`T1105 - Ingress Tool Transfer`](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1105/):** The `npm install` command transfers the malicious payload from the npm registry to the victim's machine.
- **[`T1552.004 - Private Keys`](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1552/004/):** The infostealer payload is specifically designed to steal SSH and other private keys.
- **[`T1014 - Rootkit`](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1014/):** The use of an eBPF rootkit for stealth and persistence.

## Impact Assessment
The impact on developers is potentially devastating. The theft of SSH keys, API tokens, and other credentials can lead to:
- **Compromise of Corporate Networks:** A developer's stolen SSH key could grant an attacker direct access to production servers or code repositories.
- **Further Supply Chain Attacks:** Stolen credentials for package registries (like npm or PyPI) could allow the attacker to inject malware into even more widely used software.
- **Financial Theft:** Stolen cryptocurrency wallet keys or credentials for cloud providers (like AWS) can lead to direct financial loss.
- **Loss of Intellectual Property:** Attackers could gain access to and steal proprietary source code.

## Detection & Response
1.  **Audit AUR Packages:** Developers using AUR should be extremely cautious. Before installing or updating any package, especially one that was recently orphaned and adopted, carefully inspect the `PKGBUILD` file for any suspicious commands. Look for unexpected network calls, `curl | bash` patterns, or installation of packages from other ecosystems like npm or pip. ([D3-DA: Dynamic Analysis](https://d3fend.mitre.org/technique/d3f:DynamicAnalysis))
2.  **Network Monitoring:** Monitor for unexpected outbound network connections from build processes or package installation scripts. An `install` script should generally not be making arbitrary network connections.
3.  **Check for Malicious Packages:** Search your system for the presence of known malicious npm packages like `atomic-lockfile`.
    ```bash
    npm list -g | grep atomic-lockfile
    ```

## Mitigation
1.  **Scrutinize Build Scripts:** **Never** blindly trust build scripts, even from a known package. Always review the `PKGBUILD` (or `Makefile`, `setup.py`, etc.) before building and installing software from community sources. Treat AUR helpers with caution, as they can automate away this crucial review step.
2.  **Build in Isolated Environments:** Consider building and packaging software in ephemeral, isolated environments like containers or VMs. This can limit the malware's ability to access sensitive files on your host machine. ([D3-DE: Decoy Environment](https://d3fend.mitre.org/technique/d3f:DecoyEnvironment))
3.  **Principle of Least Privilege:** Do not run build or install commands as root unless absolutely necessary. Run `makepkg` as a regular user.
4.  **Use Hardware Tokens:** Store high-value SSH and GPG keys on a hardware token (like a YubiKey). This makes it impossible for malware to simply copy and exfiltrate the key file.
